dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a Sick Person at --Address Redacted-- in [CITY]. Time out.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I have a lot of pain in my neck and lower left side of my belly from a car crash about 30 minutes ago.
Can you tell me your name and where you are today, please?
My name is Morgan. I'm in… in my car, I think. [CITY] maybe?
Tell me the date today if you can, please.
I'm not sure about the date, but I feel like I blacked out for a second… the pain in my neck is very intense.
Are you feeling comfortable breathing on your own, Morgan?
Yeah, I can breathe fine.
Respirations are 20. SpO2 is 100.
Pulse is 61 and strong, blood pressure is 125 over 82.
We haven't been able to get a glucose reading. The machine is showing zero.
The EKG is showing sinus rhythm.
I'm going to ask you a few questions now. Where were you when the crash happened, and what was going on before it?
I was in the front passenger seat of the car. There was a metal object on the road that hit our windshield. I don't remember what happened after the impact.
Is there anyone else with you right now? I see your husband and some first responders here before EMS arrived. Is that correct?
Yes, my husband is here, and some VDOT people helped me move the car off the highway after I got out of the passenger seat. I sat in the driver's seat instead.
On a pain scale from 0 to 10, how would you rate your neck and lower left abdominal pain right now, Morgan?
My neck is about a 7 and the lower left part of my belly is a 6. It's like a deep ache-not sharp or constant.
Did the pain start right when you got into the accident, or was there anything else that might have made it worse or better after?
It started right when the metal hit our car. When I tried to move the steering wheel cover to get out, my lower left side started getting more sore. The neck pain hasn't improved since.
No obvious signs of trauma to the skin.
Morgan, can I check your pupils for size and reaction to light?
Yeah, go ahead. I know I had an old injury at C3 and C4 that affects my neck.
Pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light.
I need to palpate your neck to check for tenderness. Can you let me know if it gets worse in certain areas?
Avoid the left side. My pain is mostly on the forward left side of my upper back and neck. It's flaring up with pressure.
I'd like to test the lower left side with light pressure for tenderness. Can I proceed gently?
Yes, but be really soft. It feels very tender already.
Pulse is 61, regular.
Respirations are 20, no significant distress noted.
Blood pressure is 125 over 82.
SpO2 is 100%.
Still no reading, zero on the machine.
Morgan, can you tell me what year it is and what city we are in?
I'm not sure exactly. It's 201... maybe. And [CITY]? I think.
This is 2019, and we are in [CITY]. You are alert and oriented to person, place, event, and time-A&O x4.
You have minor confusion about the date but remain otherwise alert. AMS noted.
I will secure the C-collar now for spinal protection because of your prior injury and today's incident.
That's fine. It's not causing much extra pain, though.
Using aseptic technique, I've started an 18G IV in your left antecubital with a saline lock.
We've also placed a 4-lead ECG for baseline and monitoring.
Can I check your breathing now?
Breathing sounds are clear.
SpO2 is still 100%.
Have you had anything to eat or drink prior to this incident?
I had a sandwich and a soda about 2 hours ago. After the impact, I don't think I ate or drank anything else.
Do you have any known allergies or medications we should know about?
I have an allergy to sulfa drugs. I don't take any medications currently.
We need to transport you to [COMPANY_NAME] Trauma Bay for detailed evaluation. An IV was placed for potential use during transport.
On a scale from 0 to 10, would you rate your neck and lower left abdominal pain now?
Still a 7 in the neck and a 6 in the belly.
Pulse is now 86, acceptable.
Blood pressure is 133 over 76-still within acceptable limits.
SpO2 has decreased slightly to 98%, will be monitored.
No numbness or deformity of arms or legs. Morgan stated no back pain.
Using a protocol for general trauma management, we have placed you in a cervical collar and begun 4-lead ECG monitoring.
Ready to move you now. You will be placed on the stretcher for transport.
Full straps will be used. You will be transported in semi-Fowler's position.
You are alert but slightly confused (A&O x4 with AMS). GCS is 14.
No signs of fracture on palpation of the head or neck.
Pulse: 86. Respirations: 18. BP: 133/76. Spo2: 98%. Still stable overall.
She's alert but slightly confused with a GCS of 14. C-collar in place.
Transporting hot with IV (extremity vein of left arm), EKG in place, and extracorporeal ECG monitoring.
The patient was found alert but slightly confused (GCS 14). Chief complaints are neck and lower left abdominal pain post-MVC with no obvious major bleeding or deformity.
Vitals stable. IV and EKG in place. Patient transported in semi-Fowler's with cervical collar.
